turns.text: Hello Cheri, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're in the middle of your pregnancy, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

Thanks for having me. I suppose I’m ready. It’s strange, sitting on this side of the desk. Usually, I’d be the one smoothing out the paperwork, telling a couple, "Don't worry, the heartbeat sounds perfect," while already mentally ticking off the next six things I need to cover. Now, I’m the one sitting in the plastic chair in Dennis, trying to hide how much I’m shaking while I watch the clock.

It’s… exhausting. Not just the physical part—I know the anatomy, I know what my body is doing, and I know exactly what the hormones are supposed to be spiking—but the *other* stuff. I’m constantly doing math in my head. I look at the prenatal vitamin prescription and wonder if the co-pay is going to leave me enough for gas to get to work next week. 

My doctor, she’s nice enough, I guess. But when she’s checking my iron or talking about the anatomy scan, she’s talking to me like I’m a novice. I almost corrected her on the absorption rates last week, but I stopped myself. It’s lonely, feeling like you’re just another file in a stack. Being "low risk" feels like a label they use to keep me at arm’s length, even when I’m drowning in the reality of doing this for the third time, alone. It’s hard to feel like anyone’s actually *seeing* me, you know?
Tell me about the midwives, doctors, and other healthcare professionals who were part of your pregnancy care. Which relationships felt most important to you, and why?

Honestly, the relationships feel pretty transactional, which I guess is the nature of the beast when you’re navigating the system on a budget. My doctor—she’s technically great at her job. She’s efficient, she’s quick, and she never misses a clinical beat. But there’s no room in those fifteen-minute slots for me to be anything other than a textbook example of a "healthy pregnancy." 

It’s weird, because I spent years being that person in the white coat. I know exactly what I’m looking for, and I know when a provider is just ticking boxes on a screen. When she rattles off my vitals, I sometimes have to bite my tongue to keep from finishing her sentences. I think that’s why I feel so guarded. If I let on that I know what’s coming, the human connection—what little there is—seems to vanish entirely.

I think the relationship that *would* be most important is the one I’m not getting: someone who acknowledges that my "low risk" medical status doesn't mean my life is low-stress. I don’t need someone to explain the anatomy scan to me like I’ve never seen a sonogram; I need someone to look at me and ask how I’m actually managing with three kids and a bank balance that’s perpetually hanging by a thread. That’s the kind of care that would actually feel like support. Right now, it just feels like oversight.
Tell me about your partner's — or your closest support person's — involvement during your pregnancy appointments and day-to-day experience. How did their presence or absence shape things for you?

It’s just me. That’s the reality of it. When I look around the waiting room and see other women holding hands with their partners, or whispering about baby names, I have to look down at my phone and scroll through my budget app just to keep my composure. 

Being the one who handles everything—the prenatal vitamins, the school schedules, the work shifts, the constant, nagging anxiety about the rent—it makes the appointments feel even colder. There’s nobody there to catch the things I’m too tired to process. When the doctor talks about risks or testing, there’s no one to hold my hand or ask the questions I’m too proud to voice. I’m the educator, the mother, and the breadwinner, all rolled into one. I don't have the luxury of leaning on anyone else because, well, there’s nobody to lean on.

It shapes everything. It means I go into these rooms with my armor on. If I break down, who picks up the pieces? If I get overwhelmed, the bills still have to get paid. So, I stay quiet, I listen, and I handle it. But God, sometimes I just want someone to ask me how *I’m* doing—not how the fetus is doing, or how my blood pressure is—just how I’m holding up. But that’s not really on the chart, is it?
How did that affect your relationship with each other — did pregnancy bring you closer together or create tensions?

I think there’s a bit of a misunderstanding—or maybe I wasn’t clear. When I say it’s just me, I mean it. I’m not in a relationship with the father of this baby. There isn't a partner to have tension with, or to be brought closer to, which honestly feels like a double-edged sword.

In some ways, it’s easier. I don’t have to fight with anyone about our choices or deal with someone else’s anxiety on top of my own. I don't have to defend why I'm worried about the money, or explain why I’m suddenly obsessed with the genetic screening results. I’m the sole captain of this ship, for better or worse. 

But then there are the moments like last night, when the baby was kicking so hard it actually took my breath away. I laid there in the dark, thinking, *“Did you feel that?”* and then the silence hits me, and I realize there’s nobody to share that wonder with. It’s a very specific kind of lonely. It’s not that the pregnancy created tension; it’s that the pregnancy stripped away the pretense that I could keep balancing everything on my own without it eventually taking a toll. I’m doing the work of two people, and I’m exhausted. Does that make sense? It’s not about fighting; it’s about the weight of being the only one holding the map.

Tell me about the people — friends, family, online communities — who you turned to most during your pregnancy when you had questions or worries. How did those relationships work alongside your formal care?

That’s the thing—I’m hesitant to turn to anyone. Because of my background in the office, I feel this weird pressure to be the "expert." If I call my mom or a friend with a question, they look at me like I should already know the answer. They say, "Cheri, you’ve done this twice, and you literally used to get paid to know this stuff, why are you asking me?" So, I keep it to myself. I don't want to admit that even though I know the science of the anatomy scan, I'm still terrified of what the screen might show.

I don’t really touch online communities. I’ve seen those forums—the panic, the misinformation, the judgment. My brain is already hyper-aware of every physiological "what-if" there is; I don’t need to add other people's anxieties to my own. 

It’s isolating. My formal care is clinical and distant, and my personal life is filled with people who think I’m "the one who has it all figured out." There’s no space in either of those worlds for me to just be a scared, tired twenty-four-year-old who doesn't know if she's going to make ends meet. I feel like I'm playing a role in every single interaction, and it’s getting harder to remember who I am when I'm not "the professional" or "the mother."

Tell me about a time during your pregnancy when you had to make a significant decision about a test, a procedure, or a care option. Who was involved in that decision with you, and how did each person's input land?

It was about three weeks ago, regarding the NIPT—the non-invasive prenatal testing for chromosomal abnormalities. Because of my age, the standard protocols usually suggest it's optional, but my brain—that "professional" side—was screaming at me that I needed that data. I needed to know, specifically because I’m already stretched so thin. If there were going to be extra medical needs, I had to start mentally and financially preparing *yesterday*.

I had to sit in that exam room and navigate the insurance coverage versus the out-of-pocket cost. It was just me and the doctor. I felt this intense, burning need to be taken seriously, but I also felt like a nuisance for even asking for the test since I’m "low risk." I wasn't just weighing the medical necessity; I was weighing the cost of the test against the cost of a new car seat or a month of groceries for the other two.

The doctor was clinical, telling me it was "strictly a personal choice," which is medical-speak for "we aren't going to advocate for this for you." I felt incredibly small. I didn't have a partner to turn to, and I couldn't lean on my friends because, again, they think I’m the expert. I made the decision to go ahead with it, but it wasn't a choice made with joy. It was a choice made through clenched teeth, sitting in a sterile room, feeling like I was betting the future of my family’s stability on a laboratory result. It wasn't a shared decision; it was a solitary, heavy burden that I carried out of the office in a plastic folder.
How did that affect your sense of being in control of your own care — did you feel like the decision was truly yours?

I felt like I was in control, but in the saddest way possible. It was the "control" of someone navigating a minefield—I was the one making the move, but only because I knew that if I didn't, nobody else would be looking out for me. 

It didn't feel like *empowerment*; it felt like exhaustion. There’s a big difference. In my old job, I used to see women make these choices, and I’d provide the information, the stats, and the support. I always thought that having the knowledge meant having the power. But sitting in that chair, knowing all the stats and still feeling like I was just another number in their system, I realized that knowledge is just a different kind of weight when you don’t have the resources to back it up.

I made the call because I had to, because my brain wouldn't let me sleep otherwise. But the system didn't make me *feel* like a participant. It made me feel like an obstacle that needed to be processed so they could move on to the next patient. I chose the test, sure. But I didn't choose the environment where I had to balance my baby’s health against the cost of a car seat. That part was chosen for me by a world that doesn't care if I’m an "expert" or not.

Tell me about how your relationships with people outside the healthcare system — your employer, your community, your cultural or faith community — shaped your pregnancy experience. Who was supportive, who added pressure?

My employer is definitely the biggest source of pressure. I’m currently looking for full-time work, and every interview is like a game of cat and mouse. I have to figure out how much to show and how much to hide. If I mention I’m pregnant, I see their eyes glaze over—or worse, they start calculating the "inconvenience" of me needing leave before I’ve even finished my orientation. It’s infuriating. I have years of experience, I’m competent, and I work harder than anyone I know, but I’m reduced to a "hiring risk" because of this little life inside me.

As for community? It’s pretty thin. People are "supportive" in the sense that they say, "Oh, you’re so strong, Cheri!" and "I don't know how you do it!" It’s meant to be a compliment, I think, but it’s actually really dismissive. It creates this barrier where I can’t admit I’m struggling because I’m expected to be this superhuman, independent mother. If I ask for help, it feels like I’m breaking the character they’ve cast me in. 

There’s no village. There’s just me, trying to build a stable life while dodging the judgment of employers and the hollow platitudes of acquaintances. My faith? Sometimes I find a quiet comfort in it, but mostly, I just pray for the strength to keep the lights on and the pantry stocked. My "support" system is basically just my own grit, and honestly, it’s wearing paper-thin.

Tell me about the moments in your pregnancy when your body was being examined or discussed by healthcare professionals. Who was present during those interactions, and how did their presence affect how you felt?

It’s always the same setup: me, the doctor, and maybe a medical assistant who flits in and out. Being the one on the table is such a total shift in power. When I was the one working in the clinic, I understood the clinical necessity—the need to measure the fundal height, the need to check for edema—but now, when it’s my belly being prodded, it feels like I’m being dismantled into parts. 

When they talk about my body—about my vitals, my weight gain, or the baby’s position—they use this detached, clinical shorthand. It’s "the patient is presenting with X," or "we need to monitor the anemia." It’s like they’re talking about a car that needs an oil change. Because I know the language, I’m constantly translating what they say into the "what-if" scenarios I worry about in the middle of the night. 

Their presence doesn’t make me feel cared for; it makes me feel exposed. I’m lying there on that crinkly paper, looking up at the ceiling tiles, trying to focus on anything other than the fact that someone who doesn't know me at all is making judgments about my health based on a ten-minute snapshot. I’m just a body to be processed. I miss being the person who could look someone in the eye and make them feel like a human being, not just a set of vitals to be checked off a list.
Were there times when having — or not having — someone with you in those clinical moments made a real difference to how safe or exposed you felt?

Every single time. It’s the difference between feeling like a person and feeling like a specimen. When you’re alone in that room, you’re entirely dependent on the provider’s bedside manner, and if they’re rushed or just focused on the chart, there’s no buffer. There's no one to lean over and whisper, "It’s okay," or to squeeze your hand when the ultrasound wand gets a little too cold or a little too firm. 

When I was working in the clinic, I used to see how a partner’s presence changed the energy of a room. It turned a medical procedure into a shared moment of transition. For me, that absence makes every exam feel like a performance I have to put on. I have to look composed, I have to be polite, and I have to be the "good, low-risk patient" so I don't give them a reason to rush me even more or write me off as difficult.

If I had someone there, I could just... exist. I could let my guard down, show a little vulnerability, and let them be the one to ask the follow-up questions when I’m too mentally drained to keep track. Being alone means I have to stay vigilant. I have to guard myself. It’s not just about safety; it’s about having a witness to the reality of my life, someone who knows that my "low-risk" file is actually a high-stakes balancing act.

Tell me about who was involved in helping you navigate the maternity system — booking appointments, understanding your options, knowing your rights. Who helped you find your way, and who made it harder?

I’m essentially my own navigator. Since I used to work in a clinical office, I’m the one who knows how to decipher the insurance codes and when to push for a referral. In that way, my past career is a double-edged sword—I know exactly how to game the system to get what I need, but I also know exactly how much they’re *not* telling me.

Nobody has helped me. It’s been a series of me holding a phone with one hand, checking my bank account with the other, and trying to stay calm while waiting on hold for twenty minutes just to confirm a co-pay. The system makes it incredibly hard, honestly. The scheduling is rigid, the portals are confusing if you aren't tech-savvy, and nobody explains the financial reality of these appointments until you’re already standing at the front desk with a bill in your hand.

If anything, the clinic staff makes it harder by treating me like I’m "just" a patient who doesn't understand the process. They don't realize I used to be their colleague. When I ask a pointed question, they often give me the canned, watered-down response they give everyone else. It’s infuriating. They don’t see me as a peer; they see me as a logistical hurdle. Having to fight for basic clarity while I’m already exhausted from work and parenting? It feels like the system is designed to break you down until you just stop asking questions and take whatever, or however little, you’re given.
Who else was involved in translating the system for you — a friend who had been through it, an online group, a community health worker?

Honestly? Nobody. I think that’s the hardest part to admit out loud. There isn’t a friend I can call who understands the intersection of being a former clinic worker and a current low-income patient. My friends are either in entirely different life stages or they’re struggling just as much as I am, so they’re not looking to hold my hand through the intricacies of genetic screening or insurance coverage.

I don’t trust online groups. I see how people talk on those forums—the echo chamber of anxiety, the judgment from people who don’t have to worry about their grocery budget—and it just feels like more static in my head. I already have a Ph.D. in "what-if" scenarios from my past job. I don’t need to add other people's horror stories to my own.

I’m the translator, but I’m translating for myself, and that’s a lonely place to be. Every time I get a bill or a pamphlet about a new test, I have to be the one to break it down, weigh it against the rent, and decide if it's worth the fight. Sometimes I catch myself wishing I could just sit down with a community health worker—someone who wasn't worried about the "fifteen-minute rule" or the "low-risk" box on the chart—but that feels like a luxury. In this system, you’re either high-risk and getting extra care, or you’re "fine" and left to fend for yourself. I’m just stuck in the middle, doing the math alone.

When you first started thinking about having a baby, were there particular people — a midwife, GP, or someone else — whose opinion you felt you could really rely on? What made you feel you could trust them?

I’m going to be honest with you—I don’t think I have that person right now. When I was younger, and before I really knew how the "sausage was made" in these clinics, I think I had that blind trust in the medical system. I believed that if I walked into an office, the person in the white coat was fundamentally on my side.

But after working in those offices? That trust is pretty much gone. I spent years watching how we talked about patients in the back—how we’d categorize them, how we’d roll our eyes at a patient asking "too many questions" because they were high-anxiety or "difficult." I saw the bias, the budget-driven care, and the way the "system" prioritized efficiency over humanity. So, now, when I sit in that chair as a patient, I’m not looking for someone to trust; I’m looking for someone who won't make a mistake that hurts me or the baby.

It’s a really cynical place to be, I know. It hurts to admit that the person who *should* be my anchor—my doctor—is just a technician to me. I don’t rely on her opinion because I know how limited her perspective is. I rely on my own research, my own memory, and my own intuition, even though it’s exhausting. I’m the only one I trust to actually hold the full picture of my life, my finances, and my medical needs all at once. Everyone else just sees a sliver of the truth.

Thinking back to conversations you had with healthcare professionals before you became pregnant — whether about timing, fertility, or your overall health — did you ever feel like decisions were being made for you rather than with you?

Oh, absolutely. Looking back, I can see it so clearly now—the way we used to present "options" in the clinic. It was always a nudge, a subtle way of directing a patient toward the path that was easiest for the office or the insurance company, rather than what was best for the person in the chair. We’d talk about "patient-centered care," but half the time, we were just guiding them through a pre-written script.

Now that I’m the one being steered, it’s infuriating. When I bring up a concern or ask for a test, the dismissal isn't always overt, but it’s there in the way the doctor taps her pen or the way she shifts her weight toward the door. They aren't saying "no," they’re saying "it’s not necessary for someone like you." It feels like I’m being managed, not consulted. 

I’ve had moments where I’ve wanted to scream, "I used to do your job, I know the script!" but that would only label me as a 'difficult' patient. So I stay quiet and let them dictate the flow. It’s a strange, bitter irony—I spent years contributing to a system that assumes patients aren't smart enough to handle the full picture, and now that I’m the patient, I’m the one paying the price for that arrogance. I don't feel like a partner in my own health; I feel like a subordinate.
Can you tell me a bit more about that moment? Who else was in the room, and how did it feel?

I remember it perfectly—it was during my first trimester, right when the morning sickness was at its peak. I was in a small, windowless exam room in Dennis. My doctor—a woman I’d worked with in a different capacity years ago, though I doubt she remembers me—was reviewing my initial intake. She was brisk, looking at my history and the fact that I wasn't married, and just started rattling off the "standard" prenatal screening panel.

She didn't ask me how I wanted to approach testing. She didn't ask about my baseline anxiety or my financial capacity. She just said, "For someone in your age bracket, we usually skip the more comprehensive genetic testing unless there’s a specific indication, to keep your costs down."

It felt like a gut punch. She thought she was doing me a favor—being "kind" to my wallet—but she was stripping away my agency. She didn't know I had the money saved *specifically* for that, or that the "what-ifs" were keeping me awake at night. There was only a medical assistant in the corner, typing into a laptop, not even looking up. I felt so small, like I was being handled, not heard. I didn’t push back because, in that moment, I felt like a statistic in her database—a "low-risk, low-income" patient who shouldn't be asking for "extra." It was a cold, clinical dismissal that left me feeling completely powerless.

Before you became pregnant, how did you feel about your body — particularly in relation to the idea of pregnancy and everything that comes with it? Was that something you talked about openly with anyone?

Before I got pregnant, I treated my body like a machine—or maybe more like a tool. I spent all those years in clinical settings, dissecting the biology of pregnancy, teaching couples about the "ideal" fertile window, and watching screen after screen of anatomy scans. In my mind, pregnancy was a sequence of physiological events: hormone surges, fetal growth milestones, and tissue development. I viewed my own body through that same cold, analytical lens. I thought, *if I know the mechanics, I can master the process.*

I never talked about it openly. Who would I have talked to? I was the professional, the one who was supposed to be detached and objective. I couldn't exactly sit down with my coworkers and say, "Hey, I’m terrified that if this ever happens to me, I’ll be reduced to a chart entry." 

I thought I was prepared because I knew the vocabulary. I thought I knew exactly what to expect. But actually *being* in this body, feeling it change, and watching it struggle under the weight of my life circumstances... it’s completely different. It’s not just a set of biological processes. It’s a physical, messy, exhausting reality that I’m trying to manage while simultaneously being scared out of my mind that something will go wrong. My body doesn't feel like a miracle; it feels like an asset I’m desperately trying to protect while the world around me makes it increasingly expensive to do so.
When you were searching online or using apps for information before pregnancy, how did you decide what to trust? Were there sources that felt reliable to you, and others that didn't?

Since I spent years working in a clinical office, I developed a very specific internal filter for information. I automatically gravitated toward peer-reviewed journals, ACOG (American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists) guidelines, and anything that relied on large-scale clinical trials. I trusted the raw data because, in my old job, that was the gold standard. I steered clear of "mommy blogs" and forum-based anecdotal advice because I knew, clinically, that one person’s outlier experience shouldn't be mistaken for a statistical norm.

But there’s a massive gap between trusting a source and feeling like that source *knows me*. The "reliable" sources—the textbooks, the clinical guidelines—they describe a version of pregnancy that is purely physiological. They talk about "the patient" as an abstraction. They never discuss the logistical hell of balancing a 15-minute appointment with the reality of being a low-income, single mother in Massachusetts. 

The stuff that felt "unreliable" or "scare-mongering"—the forums where people panicked about symptoms—actually felt more human. Even though I knew they were often wrong or biased, those voices at least acknowledged that pregnancy is scary. It’s a strange irony: I trusted the medical journals for the *facts*, but the medical journals offered me zero comfort or acknowledgement of my life. I had to learn the hard way that knowing the science doesn't protect you from the system.

Before you became pregnant, were there things about your body — your weight, a health condition, your age, or anything else — that felt like they might be seen as a problem or a risk by healthcare professionals? How did that feel?

Because I worked in the field, I had this constant, low-level anxiety that my body would be "read" as a liability before I even opened my mouth. I’m twenty-four and low-income, which, in the eyes of a clinical office, is basically a red flag for "high-risk" social determinants of health—even if my vitals are perfect. I knew exactly how we’d talk about a patient like me in the breakroom: "Oh, she’s young, she’s single, she’s going to need extra social work support, she’s going to be a struggle to coordinate." 

I spent so much time being the one *using* those labels that the thought of having them applied to me felt like a betrayal. It felt like I was already being pre-judged. I remember watching doctors glance at a chart and decide someone was "unreliable" or "non-compliant" based on things like their insurance type or their address. I was terrified that when I finally became a patient, they’d look at my file—the lack of a partner, the lower income bracket—and immediately start managing me with condescension instead of care.

It’s a strange kind of imposter syndrome in reverse. I’m not worried about my medical health—I’m worried about my *social* health being treated like a pathology. I felt like I had to be "the perfect patient" just to prove I wasn't the stereotype the system expected me to be. That pressure is exhausting; it’s like living under a microscope where every choice is scrutinized for "risk."
Before you were pregnant, how involved was your partner — or someone close to you — in conversations about trying for a baby, including any appointments or decisions about your health?

It’s hard to talk about this because the "trying" part... well, there wasn't a partner in the room with me. When I think back to the months leading up to finding out, it was just me sitting on my bed, calculating my basal body temperature and checking my own cervical mucus, just like I used to explain to patients in the office. 

There was no "we." There was no partner to turn to and say, "Is this the right time?" or "Are we ready for this?" There was only my internal monologue, weighing the cost of life versus the fear of being alone. I felt this intense, crushing weight of responsibility because I knew that whatever happened, I was the one who would have to bear the entirety of the consequences. I wasn't making a family plan; I was conducting a personal risk assessment.

I didn't have anyone to lean on, and in a way, I think I preferred it. If I had brought someone into that, I would have had to explain my anxiety, and I couldn’t bear the thought of someone looking at me with pity or, worse, telling me I wasn't "ready." I kept it all behind a wall. Even back then, I was already practicing how to handle this alone—pretending I had everything under control while my heart was racing, just waiting for that little plus sign to show up.
Did your partner come to any consultations with you, and if so, how did that change the dynamic in those appointments?

There is no partner to come to consultations with me. It’s just me, every time, walking into that clinic on my own. 

I’ve spent a lot of time observing what happens when a partner *does* come in. I’ve seen the way the doctor’s tone changes—they soften, they start explaining things in slightly more accessible terms, and they seem to treat the pregnant person with more... gravity, I guess. Like they’re part of a unit that needs protecting. 

When you’re alone, that dynamic is completely absent. The doctors are faster, more clinical, and frankly, more dismissive. They don't have to pause to address anyone else’s questions or concerns, so they just fly through the checklist. It makes me feel like a solo performer in a play where everyone else in the audience has a partner to talk to afterward. 

I find myself wishing I had someone there, not just for the emotional support, but for the *credibility*. There’s this weird societal bias where a woman alone is seen as a "troubled" case, whereas a woman with a partner is seen as a "family." Being alone means I have to fight twice as hard to be taken seriously, to get them to slow down, and to ensure they actually see me instead of just checking their watch. It’s an incredibly heavy, invisible burden.

Before you became pregnant, were there moments in your interactions with the healthcare system where you felt like your voice mattered — and moments where it really didn't? I'd love to hear about both, if you're comfortable sharing.

It’s such a sharp contrast. When I worked in the clinic, I felt like my voice mattered *only* when I was acting as an extension of the provider. If I was repeating the clinical script—explaining the stats, the risks, the protocols—the patients listened, and the doctors nodded along. I was an authority figure then. I was useful to the machine, so my voice had a place in the hierarchy. I felt capable, respected, even a little powerful.

But those moments where my voice *didn't* matter? Those started happening the second I began advocating for the "exceptions." Whenever I’d try to push for a more nuanced approach for a patient—like suggesting more time for a scared, low-income mother who didn't have a reliable way to get to the office—I was shut down. I’d be told, "Cheri, we have a schedule to keep," or "That’s not the standard of care." I realized then that my voice only mattered as long as it wasn't challenging the status quo.

Now, as a patient, it’s even worse. My voice feels completely muted. When I try to share my concerns about my budget or my anxieties about the "what-ifs," it’s like I’m speaking a different language. They hear "high-anxiety" or "difficult" instead of "human being with valid life stressors." I’m the same person, with the same medical knowledge, but because I’m the one in the paper gown, my expertise is suddenly invisible. It’s like I’ve been demoted from being a participant in the conversation to just being a problem to be managed.

As your pregnancy progressed, how did your sense of what you wanted from your care change — particularly around having your own wishes respected and feeling like you were genuinely in charge of decisions about your body?

At the beginning, I think I was clinging to the idea that if I just *did everything right*—if I followed the guidelines perfectly, kept my vitals in range, and didn't make a fuss—then I’d earn the respect of the system. I thought "being in charge" meant proving I was the most informed patient in the room. I wanted them to treat me like a colleague, someone they could talk to as an equal.

But as the weeks dragged on and I realized that my financial reality—my "low-income" status—meant they were never going to see me as a peer, that changed. I stopped wanting their approval and started wanting their *honesty*. 

Now, I don't care if they think I’m "smart" or "the expert." I want them to stop pretending this is just a medical event. I want to feel like I’m in charge because I’m respected, not because I’m "compliant." I’ve realized that being in control doesn't mean having a voice in the room; it means having a doctor who doesn't see my stress as an "inconvenience." I want my wishes respected in terms of my *whole life*—not just the fetus’s heart rate. I’m starting to realize that the system is set up to strip away your autonomy, and I have to fight to keep it, even if that means being the "difficult" patient they roll their eyes at.
Were there moments when you felt truly listened to and respected as the expert on your own experience — and moments when you didn't?

Honestly, I can’t think of a single moment in this office where I felt like the "expert" on my own experience. It’s almost laughable—I have the clinical vocabulary, I understand the underlying mechanics of everything they’re checking, and I can predict their next move before they make it—but that knowledge doesn't earn me a seat at the table. It’s like I’m a ghost in the room.

There’s a nurse who sometimes takes my blood pressure. She’s the only one who actually *looks* at me. She doesn't just look at the monitor; she asks, "How are you *actually* managing today?" When she asks, I feel a tiny flicker of being human, of being seen. But even then, it feels limited. She’s constrained by the same fifteen-minute timer as everyone else. 

The moments I *don't* feel listened to? Those are constant. It’s every time I’m treated as a "low-risk" case that doesn't require a real conversation. It’s the condescending way they speak to me about things I already know, or the way they dismiss my concerns about my living situation as "social work issues" that are separate from my medical care. They’ve completely partitioned my life. They want the "medical" Cheri, but they have zero room for the human Cheri. I’m the expert on my own life, but in that office, my opinion is the least valued thing on the chart.
During your pregnancy, how involved was your partner — or anyone else close to you — in decisions about your care, appointments, or birth plan? How did you feel about that level of involvement?

I feel like I’m repeating myself, but it’s just me. There isn't a partner to be involved. When it comes to the birth plan, the appointments, or the big decisions, it’s a solo operation. 

Honestly, part of me wonders if I’d even *want* someone involved at this stage. I’ve built up this fortress of independence because I’ve had to. If a partner were involved, I’d have to manage their reactions on top of my own. I’d have to explain why I’m stressed about the money, or why I’m being so meticulous about my medical record, and I just don't have the emotional bandwidth for that. I’m already carrying the weight of being the "planner," the "provider," and the "patient."

But then, there are nights when I look at my list of prenatal tasks—the appointments, the insurance hurdles, the nursery planning—and I feel this crushing realization that I am the only person who knows what it’s like to be *me* right now. Nobody else is in the room with me to see the full picture. It’s not that I want someone to take over—I don’t think I’d trust anyone else to handle it the way I do—but I crave a witness. I crave someone who understands that being a "low-risk" patient doesn't mean being a low-stress person. Right now, my level of involvement is 100% me, and it’s a lonely, heavy, and very isolating way to get through a pregnancy.
Were there times when your partner's presence in appointments changed what was said — by you, or by the healthcare professionals?

I think I've made it pretty clear there isn't a partner in the room. But let’s talk about that dynamic, because I’ve spent years watching it from the other side of the desk. When a patient brings someone with them—a partner, a sister, even a friend—the entire temperature of the room shifts. The provider suddenly speaks slower. They add more context. They offer more "we" statements: "We should consider this," or "How are we feeling about this?"

When I’m in there alone, I’m "the patient." It’s singular. It’s clinical. There’s no "we" because there’s no witness to my struggle. If I had someone there, I suspect the doctor would be forced to acknowledge that I’m a person with a life outside those four walls, not just a biological event. I’m convinced that if a partner were sitting in that chair, the doctor might actually look at *both* of us when they talk about the cost of tests or the logistical hurdles of the anatomy scan. 

Being alone makes me invisible. It allows them to bypass the messy, human stuff—the rent, the car seat, the sheer exhaustion—and treat me like a file that needs to be processed. I’m not just missing a partner for the emotional part; I’m missing a partner for the *leverage*. I’m missing the person who would force them to see that I’m more than just a low-risk chart entry.

How did your relationship with your body change during pregnancy — did you feel more connected to it, or did it bring up feelings that were harder to sit with? Was there anyone you felt safe talking to about that?

It’s a strange, dissonant feeling. On one hand, I’m intimately aware of every shift—the way my center of gravity is moving, the flutter of kicks that remind me this is real. There’s a quiet, private awe in that. But because I spent so long viewing my body as a set of biological mechanics, it feels like I’m constantly "monitoring" myself rather than just living in it. 

I don’t feel connected to it in a "glowy" way. I feel like I’m observing a host that is under a lot of pressure. My body is doing this incredible, heavy lifting—growing a life—while I’m simultaneously trying to keep it fed, housed, and working. It’s hard to sit with the fact that my body, which is working so hard, is also a source of financial stress. Every time I get a bill for an ultrasound or have to skip a meal to afford a co-pay, I feel a physical tug-of-war. 

There is nobody I feel safe talking to about this. If I mention the stress to a friend, they think I’m complaining about the baby, not the system. If I mention it to the doctor, I’m just "high-risk" or "anxious." So, I keep it inside. My body is a private, guarded space where I hold both the wonder of this new life and the crushing fear of the world we’re about to walk into together. It’s the loneliest place I’ve ever been.
Were there comments — from a healthcare professional, partner, or someone else — that affected how you felt about your changing body?

It’s usually the comments that sound like "compliments" that sting the most. You know the ones: "You’re all baby!" or "You’re barely showing, you look so healthy." People seem to think that because I’m technically "low-risk" and following the charts, my body is just a public gallery for them to comment on.

But the real, cutting comments come from the clinic. A doctor once looked at my weight gain—which was perfectly within the ACOG guidelines—and said, "We need to make sure we’re keeping this under control, we don't want any complications." It was said so casually, but it felt like a warning: *Don't get too big, don't get too expensive, don't become a problem.*

Even the silence hurts. When I’m on the exam table and they’re moving that cold gel over my stomach, they don't look at *me*; they look at the screen. They talk about the fetal heart rate or the placenta as if my body is just a container they’re poking at. It’s like they’ve forgotten that there’s a human soul inside this shifting, stretching skin.

No one has ever looked at my changing body and said, "I know how much work this is, and I see how strong you are for carrying all of this on your own." Instead, it’s just data points and shorthand. It makes me feel like my body is something I have to defend, rather than something I get to inhabit.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

I think the only thing I’d add is that I’m tired of being described as "strong." People say it all the time—"Oh, Cheri, you’re so strong, you’ve got this handled." It’s meant to be kind, but it feels like a way to look away. It’s like, if I’m "strong," they don’t have to worry about me, and they don't have to look at the gaps in the system that are making me struggle.

Being "strong" shouldn't be a prerequisite for decent maternity care. I don't want to be a hero; I just want to be a patient who is treated with the same dignity as the person sitting next to me in the waiting room who has a spouse and a bank account full of savings. 

I’m carrying a lot, sure. But I’m also just a person who’s scared. I’m scared for the baby, I’m scared for the bills, and I’m scared that when I walk into that delivery room, I’m going to be just as invisible as I am in these fifteen-minute appointments. If there’s one thing I hope your research gets across, it’s that "low-risk" medical status isn't the same thing as "low-need." We’re human beings, not just a set of stats in a folder. I just want to be seen. That’s all. Thank you for at least listening to me today. It’s the first time I’ve actually felt heard in a long time.
